rubric: See ground_truth/RUBRIC.md — label for DURATION budgeting, not literary theory.

labels.visual_event: A shabby little room. Worn red carpet. A thin pier-glass between the windows. A battered couch. On a small table: a meager scatter of coins. DELLA, about twenty, slender, in a plain worn dress, counts the money with fierce care. Brown hair pinned up. Cheeks flushed. She finishes. Counts again. And a third time.

next_beat.dialogue: One dollar and eighty-seven cents. That was all. And sixty cents of it was in pe…

labels.visual_event: Della stares at the coins. Nothing left to do. She flops down on the shabby little couch and howls-raw, helpless sobs.

prev_beat.dialogue: Three times Della counted it. One dollar and eighty-seven cents. And the next da…

next_beat.dialogue: Which instigates the moral reflection that life is made up of sobs, sniffles, an…

labels.visual_event: A letter-box into which no letter would go. An electric button that will not ring. A card: "MR. JAMES DILLINGHAM YOUNG."

next_beat.dialogue: A furnished flat at eight dollars per week.

labels.visual_event: Della finishes her cry. She attends to her cheeks with a powder rag. She stands by the window and looks out dully.
A gray cat walks a gray fence in a gray backyard.

Della whirls from the window and stands before the pier-glass. Her eyes shine brilliantly; the color drains from her face. She pulls down her hair and lets it fall to its full length. It ripples and shines like a cascade of brown waters-below her knee, almost a garment.

next_beat.dialogue: Now, there were two possessions of the James Dillingham Youngs in which they bot…

labels.visual_event: She stands very still. A tear or two splashes on the worn red carpet. Then she does the hair up again, nervously, quickly. On goes her old brown jacket. On goes her old brown hat. With a whirl of skirts and the brilliant sparkle still in her eyes, she flutters out the door.

prev_beat.dialogue: One was Jim's gold watch that had been his father's and his grandfather's. The o…

labels.visual_event: Della hurries along the cold sidewalk among Christmas shoppers, small and determined in the brown jacket and hat. She stops under a sign: "MME. SOFRONIE. HAIR GOODS OF ALL KINDS." She runs up one flight.
MADAME SOFRONIE-large, too white, chilly-waits behind the counter. Hardly the romance of "Sofronie." Della collects herself, panting.

next_beat.dialogue: Will you buy my hair?

labels.visual_event: Della removes the hat. Down ripples the brown cascade. Madame lifts the mass with a practised hand.

prev_beat.dialogue: I buy hair. Take yer hat off and let's have a sight at the looks of it.

next_beat.dialogue: Twenty dollars.

labels.visual_event: Madame works with scissors and pins. The cascade falls away. Coins clink into Della's palm-twenty dollars. Della's eyes are wet and bright. She closes her fist on the money and goes.
The next two hours on rosy wings. Della ransacks store after store-counters of watches, trays of trinkets-searching, turning things over, shaking her head, pressing on.

She finds it. A platinum fob chain, simple and chaste in design-value by substance alone, not ornament. She knows at once. It is like him. Quietness and value. She pays. Twenty-one dollars. She hurries out with eighty-seven cents left.

next_beat.dialogue: It surely had been made for Jim and no one else. It was even worthy of The Watch…

labels.visual_event: Della's intoxication gives way a little to prudence and reason. She gets out her curling irons, lights the gas, and goes to work repairing the ravages made by generosity added to love. Within forty minutes her head is covered with tiny, close-lying curls-wonderfully like a truant schoolboy. She looks at her reflection in the pier-glass long, carefully, critically.

next_beat.dialogue: If Jim doesn't kill me before he takes a second look at me, he'll say I look lik…

labels.visual_event: Seven o'clock. Coffee made. The frying-pan on the back of the stove, hot and ready to cook the chops. Della doubles the fob chain in her hand and sits on the corner of the table near the door he always enters. A step on the stair, away down on the first flight. She turns white for just a moment. Whispers:

next_beat.dialogue: Please God, make him think I am still pretty.

labels.visual_event: The door opens. JIM steps in and closes it-thin, very serious, only twenty-two. Needs a new overcoat. Without gloves. He stops inside the door, as immovable as a setter at the scent of quail. His eyes fix upon Della. An expression she cannot read. It terrifies her. Not anger, nor surprise, nor disapproval, nor horror. He simply stares, fixedly. Della wriggles off the table and goes for him.

prev_beat.dialogue: Please God, make him think I am still pretty.

next_beat.dialogue: Jim, darling, don't look at me that way.

labels.visual_event: Laboriously-as if he had not arrived at that patent fact yet.

next_beat.dialogue: Cut it off and sold it. Don't you like me just as well, anyhow?

labels.visual_event: Jim looks about the room curiously.

prev_beat.dialogue: I'm me without my hair, ain't I?

next_beat.dialogue: You say your hair is gone?

labels.visual_event: Out of his trance Jim seems quickly to wake. He enfolds his Della.

prev_beat.dialogue: Shall I put the chops on, Jim?

next_beat.dialogue: For ten seconds let us regard with discreet scrutiny some inconsequential object…

labels.visual_event: Jim draws a package from his overcoat pocket and throws it upon the table.

prev_beat.dialogue: This dark assertion will be illuminated later on.

next_beat.dialogue: Don't make any mistake, Dell, about me.

labels.visual_event: White fingers and nimble tear at the string and paper. An ecstatic scream of joy- -then a quick change to hysterical tears and wails. Jim holds her, all the comforting powers of the lord of the flat. On the table: The Combs. Side and back. Pure tortoise shell, with jewelled rims-just the shade to wear in the beautiful vanished hair.

prev_beat.dialogue: But if you'll unwrap that package you may see why you had me going a while at fi…

next_beat.dialogue: Beautiful combs that Della had worshipped long in a Broadway window.

labels.visual_event: She hugs them to her bosom. At length she looks up with dim eyes and a smile.

prev_beat.dialogue: And now they were hers, but the tresses that should have adorned the coveted ado…

next_beat.dialogue: My hair grows so fast, Jim!

labels.visual_event: Then she leaps up like a little singed cat.

prev_beat.dialogue: My hair grows so fast, Jim!

next_beat.dialogue: Oh, oh!

labels.visual_event: Jim has not yet seen his present. She holds it out eagerly upon her open palm. The dull precious metal seems to flash with a reflection of her bright and ardent spirit.

next_beat.dialogue: Isn't it a dandy, Jim? I hunted all over town to find it.

labels.visual_event: Instead of obeying, Jim tumbles down on the couch and puts his hands under the back of his head and smiles.

prev_beat.dialogue: I want to see how it looks on it.

next_beat.dialogue: Dell, let's put our Christmas presents away and keep 'em a while.

labels.visual_event: She stares at him. Then laughter breaks through the tears. She sinks beside him on the couch, the chain in one hand, the combs in the other. He draws her in. The chops wait on the stove. The little flat holds them both.

prev_beat.dialogue: And now suppose you put the chops on.

next_beat.dialogue: The magi, as you know, were wise men - wonderfully wise men - who brought gifts …

labels.visual_event: Jim and Della hold each other. The combs and the chain rest between them-useless, priceless.
